MERIDAN, Karsh Province — A magnitude 6.4 earthquake struck the mountain town of Meridan shortly before dawn on Tuesday, toppling a century-old clock tower in the central square and sending thousands of residents into the streets in the cold morning air.

The regional seismological office said the quake originated at a shallow depth of roughly eight kilometres, some twelve kilometres north-east of the town, and was felt across three provinces. At least forty aftershocks followed in the first six hours, the strongest measuring magnitude 4.8.

Emergency crews worked through the morning to clear rubble from the base of the fallen clock tower, whose brick spire had stood over the market square since 1911. Photographs from the scene showed the tower's copper clock face lying cracked among the paving stones, its hands frozen at 5:12, the moment the shaking began.

"I have lived here my whole life and I have never seen the square without that tower," said Ilse Renner, a baker whose shop faces the square. She stood in her doorway wrapped in a blanket, flour still on her apron, watching rescue workers pick through the debris.

Local officials reported no confirmed deaths by midday, though nineteen people had been treated at the district hospital for cuts and broken bones, most caused by falling masonry and shattered glass. Two apartment blocks near the square were evacuated after inspectors found long diagonal cracks running up their exterior walls.

Power was knocked out across much of the town when the tremor snapped lines along the northern ridge. Crews restored electricity to the hospital and the emergency shelter by late morning, but many homes remained dark into the afternoon.

The provincial governor arrived by helicopter around noon and toured the square on foot, stepping carefully over broken tiles. He announced that engineers from the capital would assess every building around the square before residents were allowed to return, a process he said could take several days.

By evening, volunteers had strung floodlights across the square and set up long tables of soup and bread for those unwilling or unable to go home. Children sat on the steps of the town hall, watching the empty space where the tower had stood, now marked only by a heap of brick and the twisted iron frame of the old clock.
